name: Garden & Food Storage
sourceAttribution: Compiled from USDA agricultural extension services, National Center for Home Food Preservation (UGA), USDA Plant Hardiness Zone map, and FDA food-safety guidance. NOT a substitute for tested recipes — especially for canning. Home canning errors cause botulism, which can be fatal. Always follow current NCHFP or USDA recipes precisely.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain or open license

guides.title: USDA growing zones and planting dates
guides.sourceRef: USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map (current edition) + USDA extension service planting calendars, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Your USDA zone determines what perennials survive winter and when to start annual crops.

Look up your zone: USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map. Zones run roughly 1 (Arctic) to 13 (tropical). Most US gardens are zones 4–9.

Key dates derived from your zone:
• Last spring frost (average) — when it's safe to plant tender crops outdoors (tomatoes, beans, squash).
• First fall frost (average) — gives you the days-to-maturity window for choosing varieties.

Local extension services publish planting calendars with the exact date ranges for each crop, customized for your county. These are usually free PDFs — search "[your county] extension planting calendar."

Microclimates matter. South-facing walls, urban heat, mountain valleys — all shift your effective zone by half a step or more.

guides.title: High-calorie staple crops
guides.sourceRef: USDA extension service crop calorie + storage guides, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
For calorie-per-square-foot in a self-reliance garden, these crops carry the most weight:

• Potatoes: 8–10 lb per 10-square-foot bed in good soil. Easy storage in a cool dark place. Plant from seed potatoes after last frost.
• Sweet potatoes: similar yield in warm climates (zones 7+). Need long warm season.
• Winter squash (butternut, kabocha, hubbard): high calorie density, stores 3–6 months at room temp. Needs space (vines).
• Dry beans: protein staple. Pole varieties give more per square foot than bush.
• Corn (dent or flour types, not sweet corn): grinds to meal, stores long. Needs space and good soil.
• Onions and garlic: long storage; flavor base.

For short-season climates, prioritize: potatoes, beans, kale, cabbage, beets.

A family of four growing for a meaningful portion of their food needs ~1,000+ sq ft of intensive bed space. Less for supplement only.

guides.title: Container gardening (apartments, balconies)
guides.sourceRef: USDA + state extension service container-gardening guides, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Container size matters more than people expect:
• 1–2 gal: lettuce, herbs, radishes, scallions.
• 3 gal: peppers, cherry tomatoes, dwarf bush beans.
• 5+ gal: full-size tomatoes, eggplant, dwarf zucchini, kale.
• 10+ gal: potatoes (one plant), dwarf fruit trees.

Use food-safe containers (food-grade plastic, ceramic, untreated wood, or fabric grow bags). Avoid containers that held chemicals.

Drainage holes are essential — drill them if not present.

Use a quality potting MIX (not garden soil — too heavy, compacts, may carry disease). Garden soil belongs in the ground.

Water more often than ground beds — containers dry out fast. In summer, daily or twice-daily for small pots in sun.

Feed every 2–3 weeks with diluted liquid fertilizer — nutrients leach out with each watering. Slow-release granules mixed in at planting also work.

guides.title: Canning safety — acidity is everything
guides.sourceRef: National Center for Home Food Preservation + USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning, retrieved 2026-05-26. ALWAYS use a current tested recipe — formulations have changed; old recipes can be unsafe.
guides.content:
Botulism is the danger. Clostridium botulinum spores survive in low-acid sealed jars and produce a toxin that can be fatal. Acid + heat + time are how you defeat it.

HIGH-ACID FOODS (pH 4.6 or below) — safe with WATER-BATH canning:
• Most fruits (apples, peaches, pears, berries, plums, cherries)
• Tomatoes (with added acid — modern varieties are not reliably acidic enough on their own; NCHFP standard is 2 Tbsp bottled lemon juice OR 1/2 tsp citric acid per quart jar, half that per pint)
• Pickled vegetables with adequate vinegar
• Jams, jellies, preserves

LOW-ACID FOODS (pH above 4.6) — REQUIRE PRESSURE CANNING:
• All vegetables (except properly pickled)
• Meats, poultry, seafood
• Soups, stews, sauces with meat or vegetables
• Beans, mushrooms

Water-bath canning of low-acid food is DANGEROUS. Boiling water tops out at 212°F (100°C) — not hot enough to kill botulinum spores. Pressure canning reaches 240°F+ (116°C+) under 10–15 PSI, which IS hot enough.

Rules that apply to all canning:
• Use only tested recipes from NCHFP or USDA. Old family recipes may not be safe with modern varieties.
• Use new single-use lids every time. Reusing lids fails seals.
• Process for the FULL time the recipe specifies — adjust upward for altitude per the recipe's chart.
• After cooling, check seals: lid should be concave and not flex when pressed. Refrigerate or reprocess any jar that didn't seal.
• Discard any jar that bulges, leaks, has off odors, or shoots liquid when opened. Do not taste.

guides.title: Other preservation methods
guides.sourceRef: NCHFP guides on dehydrating, freezing, and fermenting, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Freezing: simplest. Blanch most vegetables first to deactivate enzymes (2–4 min boiling water, then ice bath). Pack in moisture-proof containers. Most vegetables keep 8–12 months at 0°F (-18°C).

Dehydrating: removes water so microbes can't grow. Use a food dehydrator at 125–135°F for most vegetables, 135–145°F for meats (jerky). Test for doneness — vegetables should be brittle, fruits leathery. Store in airtight containers in cool dark place.

Fermenting: lactic acid bacteria preserve while adding flavor (sauerkraut, kimchi, pickles). 2% salt by weight is the standard ratio. Keep submerged under brine. Vegetable ferments are very safe — the acid produced inhibits pathogens. Different from canning: ferments are LIVE; refrigerate after the active phase.

Smoking: combines slow heat + smoke. Hot smoking (above 145°F) cooks AND preserves. Cold smoking (below 90°F) flavors only — meat must be fully cured first to be safe.

Curing (salt + sometimes nitrites): traditional meat preservation. Requires precise ratios for safety. Beginners should use tested cure mixes per package directions — homemade cures have killed people.

guides.title: Root cellar basics
guides.sourceRef: USDA extension service root-cellar references, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Ideal conditions: 32–40°F (0–4°C), 85–95% humidity, dark, well-ventilated to disperse ethylene (which ripens fruit too fast).

A real root cellar is a dedicated underground space. Substitutes:
• An unheated basement room with insulation against the warm wall.
• A buried trash can lined with straw, in a dug pit (apple/carrot "clamp").
• An insulated cooler in an unheated garage in winter.

Not all crops want the same conditions:
• Potatoes, carrots, beets, parsnips, turnips, cabbage: cold + humid (32–40°F, high humidity).
• Winter squash, pumpkins: cool + dry (50–55°F, 50–70% humidity).
• Onions, garlic: cool + dry (32–50°F, low humidity, well-ventilated).
• Apples: cold (32°F), separate from other crops because they release ethylene.

Check stored produce weekly. "One bad apple spoils the barrel" is literal — remove anything bruised or rotting immediately.

Label and date everything. Use oldest first.

guides.title: Shelf-stable pantry — rotation and shelf life
guides.content:
First-in, first-out (FIFO) rotation: new purchases at the back, oldest in front. Label with purchase date if packaging doesn't show one.

Long shelf-life staples (in original packaging, cool and dry):
• White rice: 25–30 years
• Dry beans: 25–30 years
• Wheat berries (whole grain): 25–30 years
• Rolled oats: 25–30 years
• Salt, sugar, honey: indefinitely
• Pasta: 5–8 years
• Peanut butter: 1–2 years
• Canned vegetables and meats: 2–5 years (use by, then test) — discard any with bulging, rust, or off smell
• Powdered milk: 2–5 years (retail packaging); up to 10 years (nitrogen-packed #10 can or mylar + O2 absorber)

Extended shelf life via mylar bags + oxygen absorbers: grains and beans can last decades. Seal in food-grade mylar with 300–500cc oxygen absorber per 1-gallon bag — use the higher end for low-density items (oats, pasta, dehydrated vegetables) which trap more air.

Rotation pattern for a 1-year supply: replace 1/12 each month so nothing exceeds expected shelf life.

For an emergency-only stash, choose foods that need no cooking and the family will actually eat. Calorie density matters; nutritional balance matters more than people think over weeks-to-months.
